items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript: Think about how a composer works today versus two centuries ago. Before recording existed, music lived only in the moment of performance and in written notation. A composer imagined sounds, wrote them on paper, and simply hoped that performers would realize them faithfully. But the invention of sound recording in the late nineteenth century changed that relationship in a fundamental way. Suddenly a performance could be captured, replayed, and studied, and this gave composers a kind of feedback they had never possessed. Consider what that meant in practice. A composer could now hear exactly how an arrangement sounded, notice a muddy passage, and revise it before the piece was ever published. Recording also let musicians study the greatest players in the world, picking up subtle techniques that notation could never capture on the page. Over time, this reshaped composition itself. Popular musicians in the twentieth century began composing directly in the recording studio, layering one track over another instead of writing a finished score in advance. The studio became an instrument in its own right. Effects like echo, overdubbing, and multitracking were not performance techniques at all; they existed only on tape. A single song might be assembled from dozens of separate takes that no ensemble could ever play live in one room. So recording did far more than preserve music. It reshaped how music was conceived, blurring the old line between composing and performing, and handing artists tools that earlier composers could scarcely have imagined.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How recording technology changed the way music is composed
options.B: The early history of written musical notation
options.C: How to operate a modern recording studio
options.D: The greatest performers of the twentieth century

questions.explanation: 全篇讲录音技术如何改变作曲方式，A 概括最准确。B、C、D 都只是讲座中提到的细节或无关话题，不能概括主旨。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention echo and overdubbing?

options.A: To describe common live performance techniques
options.B: To show that the studio created effects impossible in live performance
options.C: To explain how musical notation is written down
options.D: To list the daily tasks of a working composer

questions.explanation: 教授说这些效果“只存在于磁带上”，用来说明录音棚创造了现场无法实现的效果，故 B 对。A 与原文相反；C、D 与该例子无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what new ability did recording give composers?

options.A: The ability to perform without any rehearsal
options.B: The ability to abandon written notation entirely
options.C: The ability to hear and revise a piece before publishing it
options.D: The ability to tour to more distant cities

questions.explanation: 原文说作曲者能先听到编排、发现问题并在出版前修改，故 C 对。A、D 未提及；B 夸大，录音并未彻底取消记谱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did some twentieth-century popular musicians compose?

options.B: By deliberately avoiding the recording studio
options.C: By writing a finished score before recording
options.D: By layering tracks directly in the studio

questions.explanation: 原文说流行音乐人直接在录音棚里一轨叠一轨地作曲，故 D 对。C 与原文相反；A、B 均未提及。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the role of patronage in shaping Baroque art
items.transcript: Baroque art of the seventeenth century is often admired for its drama, its rich color, and its sheer emotional force. But to understand why it looks the way it does, you have to look at who was paying for it. Almost no artist of the period worked freely for an open market. Instead, painters and sculptors depended on patrons, wealthy and powerful clients who commissioned particular works for particular purposes. The two greatest patrons were the Catholic Church and the royal courts of Europe. After the Protestant Reformation, the Church wanted art that would move ordinary worshippers, art that was vivid, immediate, and easy to feel. That demand directly shaped the Baroque style. Dramatic lighting, twisting figures, and intense expressions were not merely artistic choices; they served the Church's goal of inspiring devotion. A painting of a saint was meant to make the viewer feel the saint's suffering, not simply admire a pretty composition. Royal patrons had different aims. A king commissioning a grand ceiling wanted to display his own power and glory, so artists produced enormous, spectacular works celebrating their patrons' status. Here is the key point: the patron usually dictated the subject, the size, and even the materials. The artist's genius lay in what he could achieve within those tight constraints. So when we look at a Baroque masterpiece, we are seeing not only an individual vision but also the desires of the institution or ruler who paid for the work.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The specific techniques used to paint Baroque lighting
options.B: How patronage shaped the subjects and style of Baroque art
options.C: The political history of the Protestant Reformation
options.D: How to tell an authentic painting from a forgery

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是赞助人如何塑造巴洛克艺术的题材与风格，B 对。A 只是细节；C、D 与主旨无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the Catholic Church want from art after the Reformation?

options.A: Art that avoided all religious subjects
options.B: Art that faithfully copied medieval styles
options.C: Art that would move and inspire ordinary worshippers
options.D: Art that displayed the power of a king

questions.explanation: 原文说教会想要能打动普通信徒、生动而直接的艺术，故 C 对。A 相反；B 未提及；D 是王室赞助人的目标。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention a king commissioning a grand ceiling?

options.A: To criticize the wasteful spending of royalty
options.B: To explain the technical steps of painting a ceiling
options.C: To argue that kings disliked the Church
options.D: To illustrate how royal patrons used art to display power

questions.explanation: 这个例子用来说明王室赞助人借艺术彰显权力，故 D 对。A、C 是无根据的推断；B 只是字面复述。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about Baroque artists?

options.A: Their creativity operated within limits set by their patrons
options.B: They generally ignored their patrons' wishes
options.C: They sold most of their works on an open market
options.D: They preferred to make small, private paintings

questions.explanation: 原文说艺术家的天才体现在“在严格限制内”能做到什么，可推出 A。B、C 与原文相反；D 无依据。

items.subject: history
items.topic: how ice harvesting shaped nineteenth-century commerce
items.transcript: So, before refrigerators existed, how did people keep food cold in summer? The answer, for much of the nineteenth century, was natural ice, and harvesting it became a surprisingly large industry. In winter, workers went out onto frozen lakes and rivers in the northern United States and sawed the ice into huge, uniform blocks. These blocks were hauled ashore, packed in sawdust to slow melting, and stored in insulated icehouses until the warmer months. Now, here is what makes this interesting. That ice was not just used locally. It was shipped astonishing distances, packed into the holds of sailing ships bound for the southern states, the Caribbean, and even India. A block cut from a pond in Massachusetts might end up cooling a drink in Calcutta months later. The trade created whole networks of cutters, shippers, and merchants, and it made some entrepreneurs quite wealthy. Why does this matter beyond a curious footnote? Because cheap ice transformed daily life. It allowed fresh meat, fish, and dairy to travel farther and last longer, which changed what ordinary people could eat. It supported the growth of industries like brewing and meatpacking. And it accustomed people to expect cold drinks and fresh food year-round. When mechanical refrigeration finally arrived, it did not create the demand for cold; the ice trade had already built it. In a sense, the frozen lakes of the north quietly reshaped commerce across the entire globe.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the first mechanical refrigerators were invented
options.B: The scientific reasons that lakes freeze in winter
options.C: How the natural ice trade shaped nineteenth-century commerce
options.D: The design and construction of sailing ships

questions.explanation: 全篇讲天然冰贸易如何重塑十九世纪商业，C 对。A、B、D 都不是主旨。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention a drink being cooled in Calcutta?

options.A: To describe the local cuisine of India
options.B: To explain the process of cutting ice
options.C: To argue that most of the ice was wasted
options.D: To illustrate how far the harvested ice was shipped

questions.explanation: 这个例子说明冰被运到极远的地方，故 D 对。A、C 是无根据的推断；B 只是字面内容。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say that mechanical refrigeration 'did not create the demand for cold'?

options.A: To emphasize that the ice trade had already built that demand
options.B: To claim that early refrigerators did not work well
options.C: To describe how a refrigerator actually functions
options.D: To praise the achievements of modern engineers

questions.explanation: 这句话强调需求早由冰贸易培养起来，故 A 对。B、D 是无根据的推断；C 只是字面。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about food before the ice trade developed?

options.A: It was always shipped over very long distances
options.B: It could not travel as far or stay fresh as long
options.C: It was generally colder than food is today
options.D: It was mostly imported from India

questions.explanation: 原文说冰让食物能走更远、存更久，可推出此前食物做不到，B 对。A、C 与逻辑相反或夸大；D 无依据。

items.subject: psychology
items.transcript: Have you ever made a difficult choice and then suddenly found yourself convinced it was obviously the right decision? That everyday experience points to one of the most influential ideas in psychology: cognitive dissonance. The concept was developed by Leon Festinger in the nineteen fifties. Dissonance is the uncomfortable tension we feel when we hold two conflicting beliefs, or when our actions clash with our attitudes. The key claim is that this discomfort motivates us to reduce it, and often we do so by changing our attitudes rather than our behavior. Let me give you the classic experiment. Festinger had participants perform an extremely boring task, then paid some of them a large sum and others only a tiny sum to tell the next person that the task was enjoyable. You might expect the well-paid people to like the task more. In fact, the opposite happened. Those paid only a little later reported that the task really was fun. Why? The ones paid a large amount had an easy justification for lying; the money explained their behavior. But those paid almost nothing had no such excuse, so to resolve the tension between saying it was fun and knowing it was dull, they unconsciously convinced themselves the task had genuinely been enjoyable. This finding surprised many people because it reversed the common assumption that bigger rewards produce stronger effects. Cognitive dissonance shows that when external justification is weak, we quietly rewrite our own attitudes to restore a sense of consistency.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How to design a controlled psychology experiment
options.B: Why so many laboratory tasks are boring
options.C: The personal life and career of Leon Festinger
options.D: Cognitive dissonance and how it changes people's attitudes

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是认知失调及其如何改变态度，D 对。A、B、C 只是细节或无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is cognitive dissonance?

options.A: The discomfort of holding conflicting beliefs or acting against one's attitudes
options.B: The pleasure of making an easy, quick decision
options.C: A method for deciding how much to pay participants
options.D: A particular type of boring laboratory task

questions.explanation: 原文定义失调为持有冲突信念或行为与态度不符时的不适感，A 对。B 相反；C、D 曲解。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Festinger's experiment in detail?

options.A: To explain how participants can earn money in studies
options.B: To illustrate how weak justification leads people to change their attitudes
options.C: To restate the earlier definition of dissonance
options.D: To argue that boring tasks are harmful to people

questions.explanation: 实验用来说明外部理由弱时人会改变态度，B 对。A、D 是无根据推断；C 只是复述。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about people who receive a large reward for saying something they do not believe?

options.A: They will always tell the complete truth afterward
options.B: They feel much more dissonance than everyone else
options.C: They feel less need to change their real opinion
options.D: They end up enjoying the task more than anyone

questions.explanation: 重赏为撒谎提供了理由，因而不太需要改变真实看法，C 对。A、D 夸大；B 与逻辑相反。
